title: Raku firing: outdoor process
subtitle: Removing red-hot glazed pots from a small kiln and post-firing reduction
excerpt: Raku firing involves glazing bisqued work with raku-specific glazes, firing rapidly to around 950°C in a small kiln, and then removing the glowing pieces with tongs and placing them in a metal bin filled with combustible material. The reduction atmosphere created in the bin produces characteristic metallic lustre and carbon-blackened unglazed surfaces.
sourceNotes: Western raku firing technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fast, low-temperature firing process where red-hot pieces are removed from the kiln at peak temperature and placed in a reduction container. Western raku produces distinctive metallic and carbon effects.
An oxygen-deficient atmosphere in the kiln or post-firing container. Reduction withdraws oxygen from metal oxides in the glaze, changing their colour and creating metallic effects.
The stress caused by rapid temperature change in ceramic work. Raku clay bodies are formulated with high grog content to withstand the extreme thermal shock of the raku process.

content.text: is an outdoor process. You need protective clothing, a clear working area, and a fire extinguisher within reach. The pieces must use a high-grog raku clay body to handle the extreme
of removal from a hot kiln. The
atmosphere in the post-firing container produces the characteristic effects: metallic lustre in glazed areas and carbon black in the unglazed clay.

attrs.content: Raku involves handling ceramics at around 950°C with tongs. Work outdoors in a clear area free of flammable materials at ground level. Wear a studio apron, heat-resistant gloves, and an N95 mask throughout. Keep a charged fire extinguisher within 2 metres of the work area. Never work alone. The reduction bin contents (newspaper, sawdust) will combust — this is intentional, but must be controlled.

items.name: Bisque-fired raku clay pieces, glazed with raku glaze
Small electric or propane raku kiln
Heavy-duty metal bin with lid (20-30 L)
Combustible material: newspaper or dry sawdust

content.text: Apply raku glaze to bisqued pieces by brushing or dipping, to a thicker coat than standard glazing, approximately 2 mm. Leave the foot ring and lower 15 mm of the base unglazed; these areas will turn carbon-black. Common raku glazes include copper-metallic glazes that produce a shiny coloured surface in reduction, and crackle white glazes that develop fine cracks filled with carbon.

Load the glazed pieces onto the kiln shelf with space between them. Fire rapidly to 900-950°C. Raku firing is much faster than bisque or glaze firing. A small raku kiln typically reaches temperature in 20-40 minutes. Watch the glaze surface through the peephole: the glaze is ready when it becomes shiny and molten-looking, and the glaze surface shows a smooth, reflective sheen.
Place a large handful of crumpled newspaper in the bottom of the metal bin.
With gloves and apron on, open the kiln and lift the glowing piece out with the tongs in a smooth motion. The piece should be glowing orange-red.
Set the piece into the metal bin on top of the newspaper. The newspaper will ignite immediately from the heat of the ceramic.
Lid the bin immediately. Allow the reduction process to run for 10-15 minutes with the lid sealed.
Remove the lid with tongs and lift the piece out. Quench in a bucket of water (produces dramatic steam) or allow to cool in air.
Leave the piece in the reduction container for longer. This drives more carbon into the unglazed clay.
Try horse-hair instead of newspaper. Lay strands on the hot piece right after it comes out of the kiln. The hair burns on contact and leaves dark linear marks on the surface.

items.symptom: Piece cracks on removal from the kiln
items.cause: The clay was not a raku-formulated body with enough grog. Standard stoneware cannot handle the thermal shock.
items.fix: Always use a dedicated raku clay body. Do not attempt this process with standard throwing or hand-building stoneware

items.symptom: Metallic glaze effects patchy and dull after reduction
items.cause: Not enough combustible material was in the bin. The reduction atmosphere was not dense enough.
items.fix: Use a larger handful of newspaper and ensure the bin is sealed tightly for the full 15 minutes
